paper_title: A deep learning approach for predicting linear accelerator output settings in automated radiotherapy planning of oligometastatic cancer.
journal: Physics and imaging in radiation oncology
raw_limitation_text:
[DISCUSSION]
Combining all samples of each anatomical site to increase the sample size to train only one model provided satisfactory accuracy in the deep learning prediction of MU per CP of ablative hypofractionated radiotherapy of metastases. This strategy reduced the required computational resources and labour as compared with the case where models would have been trained for each anatomical site. Interestingly, massively augmented data used for training single-site models also provided satisfactory accuracy for the prediction of MU per CP. These findings are clinically significant to support same-day radiotherapy and to facilitate the planning of multiple metastases. It is worth noting that since the dose magnitude is proportional to the prescription dose and the MU per beam are proportional to the dose per fraction, many fractionation types were rescaled for training. The site with a single fractionation yielded the same level of accuracy as sites with multiple fractionations, validating our method. Furthermore, our approach is generalizable to diverse populations because the dose used as input is proportional to the patient attenuation. Based on our results, our method to predict the MU per CP can be integrated into the AI VMAT planning workflow to improve dose-volume metrics and dose conformity.

In each case considered, the dose-volume metrics and dose conformity obtained with AI-RTPlans were improved or comparable to the clinical dose-volume metrics and conformity. Similar results were obtained in the prediction of the MU per CP in lung cancer performed by our research group, for which the arc length was varied. These results suggest that the network architecture is robust in terms of anatomical site, prescription dose, and fractionation. Furthermore, in this current study, the agreement between calculated doses in AI-RTPlans and clinical plans was superior to an earlier study in conventional radiotherapy of prostate (average/minimum gamma passing rate 3%/3 mm = 98.8%/81.9% over all results of this study versus 92.0%/70.0% in the study for prostate cancer). It is worth noting that this current study only focused on the prediction of the MU per CP whilst the early study predicted both MLC leaf positions and MU per CP, which was more challenging.

The number of samples considered in this study was sufficient to predict the MU per CP accurately, but was obtained from a relatively small number of patients per site. Although data augmentation proved to be sufficient in this context, a larger patient cohort for each site may have provided more accurate predictions. The number of arcs was the same across all plans considered in this study. Further investigation into the effect of varying the number of arcs, or with intensity modulated radiotherapy plans, would be interesting. Furthermore, the results shown in this work were achieved with the most performant hyperspace parameter combination of the network. Other convolutional neural networks, such as the ResNet, or transformer-based network, or recurrent neural networks with long-short term memory layers able to consider the input’s temporal dependence, may have achieved higher accuracy in this context. Moreover, the workflow introduced in this work assumed that dose distributions per CP were available before training. In the routine clinical planning of AI VMAT, these dose distributions would be predicted for MLC leaf positions extraction, and therefore, the accuracy of the methods introduced in this work would depend on the accuracy of these predictions. Although AI VMAT planning is promising, several challenges hinder its clinical implementation. Consequently, improving the prediction accuracy of organ segmentation, 3D dose distribution, MLC leaf positions, and MU per CP is essential for widespread adoption.

Katrina Woodford: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jason Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Susan Harden: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Sandro Porceddu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Nicholas Hardcastle: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Vanessa Panettieri: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

[CONCLUSION]
Augmenting data extensively and combining anatomical sites were equivalent and proficient strategies to predict machine settings for radiotherapy planning of oligometastatic cancer.

Introduction

Hypofractionated or ablative radiation doses are beneficial in radiotherapy of oligometastatic cancer. Since a large amount of dose is delivered to the tumour during these treatments, a steep dose gradient and a strategy for avoiding organs at risk (OAR) are necessary to reduce the dose received by adjacent healthy organs. This complex spatial dose distribution is achieved by the automated variation of machine settings during dose delivery. The values of the machine settings are determined at the treatment planning stage and stored in control points (CP). Their patterns result from a complex iterative mathematical calculation that optimises the dose distribution by varying the machine settings. In volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT), this iterative process may take from hours to days to achieve an acceptable solution, which delays treatment and requires additional human resources. Given the often relatively simple target-organ geometric relationship in metastatic cancer, an automated workflow is clinically relevant for efficient treatment planning of multiple metastases, and to support same-day radiotherapy.

Automated treatment planning may be achieved with deep learning prediction of machine settings. Deep learning is a subset of artificial intelligence (AI) where models are trained from datasets to perform predictions. Artificial intelligence VMAT planning aims to produce deliverable treatment plans in a few minutes with minimal input and without dose optimisation. This concept is at its early stage, and pioneer studies have demonstrated its complexity. One promising approach involves independent networks connected by their input/output, sequentially predicting organ segmentations, three-dimensional dose distribution, multileaf collimator (MLC) leaf positions per CP, and monitor units (MU) per CP. The feasibility of this approach has been demonstrated for prostate cancer. In this concept, each network can be developed and improved independently of other networks in the chain. Therefore, strategies to improve the prediction accuracy of each constituent element are beneficial as they directly translate to improvements in the prediction accuracy of the overall assembly.

The MU are the linear accelerator outputs during delivery and can be predicted with deep learning. The MU per CP control the dose magnitude and affect the gantry speed and meterset rate during delivery. Accurate MU per CP prediction is therefore essential in AI VMAT planning, especially for stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy (SABR) due to the necessary steep dose gradient around the target.

Moreover, deep learning typically requires large datasets to generate accurate predictions, and models are generally trained by anatomical sites. However, due to the relatively limited frequency of radiotherapy for oligometastatic cancer, the available clinical sample size may be relatively small, even in high-volume centres.

We aim to introduce deep learning methods to predict the MU per CP in hypofractionated and ablative radiotherapy of liver, bone, and lung metastasis. We further aim to compare two approaches to improve prediction accuracy by increasing the sample size, namely massively augmenting data for a single site and combining all sites during training.

Materials and methods

Treatment plans

Patient characteristics. M: Male; F: Female; O: Other; Fx: Fractions; MU: Monitor units; FFF: Flattening filter free.

Liver	Bone	Lung	 		Number of plans				 			40	68	88	 		Number of patients				 			38	65	78	 		Gender				 		M	22	40	44	 		F	16	25	32	 		O	0	0	2	 		Age at treatment				 		median (min, max) [years]	61 (23, 84)	64 (30, 83)	68 (19, 88)	 		Gross tumour volume				 		Volume average (min, max) [cm3]	24.1 (0.3, 298.4)	9.6 (0.1, 68.9)	4.8 (0.1, 25.2)	 		Number of treatment plans per fractionation				 		40 Gy/5Fx	4	0	0	 		45 Gy/5Fx	3	0	0	 		50 Gy/5Fx	7	0	0	 		24 Gy/2Fx	0	17	0	 		42 Gy/3Fx	8	0	0	 		48 Gy/3Fx	16	0	0	 		54 Gy/3Fx	2	0	0	 		20 Gy/1Fx	0	47	0	 		24 Gy/1Fx	0	4	0	 		28 Gy/1Fx	0	0	88	 		Number of treatment plans per total number of control points				 		208	24	40	56	 		228	16	28	32	 		Energy and meterset rate				 		10 MV-FFF-2400 MU/min	39	49	0	 		6 MV-FFF-1400 MU/min	1	17	87	 		10 MV-600 MU/min	0	1	0	 		6 MV-600 MU/min	0	1	1

Consecutive patients treated between 01/2019 and 06/2024 at our institution for oligometastatic disease to the liver, bone, and lung were considered. The patient characteristics are shown in Table 1. This study was approved by the Human Research Ethics Committee of the Peter MacCallum Cancer Centre (HREC/101771/PMCC). The prescription dose ranged between 8 Gy and 28 Gy per fraction and was delivered in one to five fractions. Treatment plans were included if they had 208 or 228 CP distributed in two arcs. Either the standard or high-definition MLC, with 5 mm or 2.5 mm inner leaf width, respectively, was used. The treatments were delivered with a VMAT technique by TrueBeam linear accelerators (Varian Medical Systems, USA). Dose optimisation and dose calculation were performed with the Eclipse treatment planning system (v16, Varian Medical Systems) by using the Photon Optimizer Algorithm and the AcurosXB algorithm reporting dose to medium, respectively.

Study design

(Top) Workflow of this research project. The liver, bone, and lung single-model was trained and used on a liver, bone, and lung testing dataset, respectively. All samples were merged to train the multi-site model and used on the same liver, bone, and lung testing dataset. (Bottom) Illustration of the network architecture, an encoder followed by densely connected layers. The inputs were two-dimensional average dose intensity projections (ADIP), and the outputs were the MU per CP.

The workflow of this study is shown in Fig. 1. A model was generated for each of the three anatomical sites (liver/bone/lung single-site model). Furthermore, a model was generated by combining all anatomical sites for training (multi-site model). The liver, bone, and lung models were respectively tested on a liver, bone, and lung testing dataset unseen during training. The multi-site model was applied to the same three testing datasets. Predictions from both approaches, single-site versus multi-site, were compared.

Preprocessing

The MU per CP were predicted from the two-dimensional (2D) average dose intensity projection (ADIP) per CP. The MU per CP used in clinical plans were extracted from a method devised and tested by our group. The workflow used to generate ADIPs is shown in section 1 of the supplementary material. Each control point’s contribution to the clinical dose distribution was obtained by using the BeamSplitter and was cropped to a cube of size 64 × 64 × 64 voxels centred at the centroid of a structure made of all voxels of the clinical dose with dose ≥ 70% of the maximum. The box was rotated to the 0° angle and averaged along the beams-eye view direction to produce an image of size 64 × 64 voxels. To homogenise the dataset, ADIPs were normalised by the prescription dose and rescaled to a generic prescription. The generic prescription was 48 Gy in three fractions, 20 Gy in one fraction, and 28 Gy in one fraction for training the liver, bone, and lung single-models, and 28 Gy in one fraction for training the multi-site model. The factor used to scale ADIPs was applied to the MU per CP, which were further rescaled by the number of fractions and normalised by the generic fractionation. In this context, a sample refers to a pair of ADIP and MU per CP.

Network architecture

The network architecture is shown in Fig. 1. An encoder with four downsampling steps followed by five densely connected layers with a decreasing number of neuron units was used. The downsampling step involved a 2D convolution layer with a 3 × 3 kernel, a linear activation layer, and a 2D maximum pooling layer with a 2 × 2 pooling window. The filter depth was 16 in the first step and was increased by a factor of two at each downsampling step. The training and inference were performed with NVIDIA Tesla V100 graphics processing units (GPU) with 32 GB of random-access memory. Training was optimised with the Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam). The learning rate was initially set to 10-3 and decreased by a factor of 0.2 after every five epochs without improvement in the loss function up to a value of 10-6. Based on empirical testing, the training was automatically stopped after 50 epochs without improvements in the loss function. The loss function used was the mean absolute error. The best model achieved during training was saved and used for model testing. Performance of models was assessed with the absolute percentage error (APE). The network architecture is further detailed in section 2 of the supplementary material.

Data augmentation and k-fold strategy

The sample sizes used in each training/validation/testing dataset of all models are shown in Fig. 1. To increase the size of the training dataset, each raw sample was rescaled by a normally distributed random number with mean 100 and variance 10. The training samples were augmented by a factor of 82/34/24 in the liver/bone/lung single-site model and by a factor of 12 in the multi-site model. As a result, all sample sizes used for training were within ± 1% of each other. The testing datasets involved the same number of plans in each anatomical site (20 plans, 4,320 raw samples). The same samples used for testing the single-site approaches were used for testing the multi-site approach. Furthermore, the k-fold strategy was used with k = 4 (training/validation = 75%/25% of the total number of samples used for training and validation) to improve the accuracy of each model. The results of each fold were averaged to return a single output.

Postprocessing

The predicted MU per CP were rescaled back from the generic prescription to the clinical prescription. The MU per beam were obtained by summing each contribution per CP. The meterset weight per CP was obtained by normalising the predictions by the MU per beam. The accuracy of the MU per beam and meterset weight per CP was tested with the mean and standard deviation (SD) of the APE. The predicted MU per beam and meterset weights were used to create an AI radiotherapy plan (AI-RTPlan). All other machine settings used clinically were retained. The resulting AI-RTPlans were then imported into the treatment planning system for dose calculation.

Dose evaluation

Following institutional protocols, dose distributions generated with AI-RTPlans were normalised to improve the number of objectives meeting their clinical goals. The normalised dose distributions generated by the AI-RTPlans were compared to the clinical dose distributions with gamma testing by using the PyMedPhys module (v0.39.3) with 3%/2 mm criteria and 10% dose threshold. Dose-volume metrics, conformity indices (CI100 and CI50), and clinical goals (shown in the supplementary material (Tables S1-S3) for each anatomical site) obtained with each model were compared to their clinical counterparts. AI-RTPlans and clinical plans were judged to be equivalent based on the number of objectives meeting their clinical goals. Results obtained with the liver/bone/lung model were further compared to the results obtained with the multi-site model to assess any differences.

Statistical analysis

Statistical significance was evaluated by using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test and its associated p-value. The null hypothesis was rejected when the p-value was smaller than 0.05. The statistical analysis was performed with Python (v.3.8).

Results

Distribution of the MU per CP for the three sites considered. All samples in the training/validation/testing dataset were considered.

Comparison between predicted and clinical meterset weight (msw) per control point obtained with single-site (top) and multi-site (middle) models. (bottom) Comparison between predicted and clinical beam monitor units obtained from the single-site and multi-site models. Results for liver (left), bone (middle), and lung (right) are shown for the 20 plans in the testing dataset.

The clinical MU per CP distributions for all plans considered are shown in Fig. 2. The clinical MU per CP were mean ± SD (min, max) = 15.6 ± 8.1 MU (0.1, 108.0) MU/20.9 ± 10.3 MU (0.1, 103.5) MU/30.5 ± 11.9 MU (0.4, 113.1) MU in the liver/bone/lung site. The times to generate AI-RTPlans were less than 5 s per patient in all models. The predicted and clinical meterset weight per CP are shown in Fig. 3 (top and middle). In liver and lung, the APE was slightly lower in single-site models as compared with the multi-site model (APE mean ± SD = 5.8 ± 6.8%/5.9 ± 6.6% and 5.9 ± 7.1%/6.2 ± 7.3% in liver and lung single-site/multi-site models, respectively, p-values < 0.03 in both cases). In bone, no statistical difference was observed between the single-site and multi-site model predictions (APE mean ± SD = 5.4 ± 6.0%/5.4 ± 6.2% in bone single-site/multi-site models, p-value > 0.1).

The predicted and clinical MU per beam are shown in Fig. 3 (bottom). In all cases, mean APEs were smaller than 5.4%. In liver, predictions were more accurate with the single-site model as compared with the multi-site model (APE mean ± SD = 4.8 ± 4.9%/5.3 ± 4.0% in liver single-site/multi-site models, p-values < 0.01). The opposite was observed in bone, predictions were more accurate with the multi-site model as compared with the single-site model (APE mean ± SD = 5.4 ± 3.7%/5.3 ± 4.0% in bone single-site/multi-site models, p-values < 0.02). In lung, no statistical difference was observed between the single-site and multi-site model predictions (APE mean ± SD = 4.6 ± 4.4%/5.2 ± 4.3% in lung single-site/multi-site models, respectively, p-value > 0.3).

Comparison of dose distributions (%) used clinically and generated with single-site and multi-site models. The axial, coronal, and sagittal views at the isocentre are shown for the patient with the lowest gamma passing rate in the liver (top), bone (middle), and lung (bottom) testing dataset.

Boxplots (outliers included) of selected dose-volume ([Gy] or [% of prescribed dose]) or volume ([cm3] or [% of total volume]) metrics evaluated in the liver (left), bone (middle), and lung (right) datasets. Clinical results (+) are compared with the single-site model (×) and multi-site model (•). Objective limits are indicated with red dashed lines. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The normalisation values and gamma passing rates are shown in the supplementary material (Tables S4-S6) for each case. An illustration of dose distributions used clinically and generated with the single-site and multi-site models is shown in Fig. 4 for the patient with the lowest gamma passing rates in the liver/bone/lung testing dataset. All sites considered, the gamma passing rates were mean ± SD (min, max) = 98.4 ± 3.1% (84.7%, 100.0%) by using predictions of single-site models and mean ± SD (min, max) = 98.2 ± 4.2% (76.9%, 100.0%) by using predictions of the multi-site model. No statistical differences were observed between gamma passing rates comparing AI-RTPlan and clinical plan of each single-site model versus the multi-site model (p-value > 0.09 in each of the three comparisons). The comparison between selected dose-volume metrics is shown in Fig. 5 and further detailed in the supplementary material (Tables S7-S9). In all cases, all AI-RTPlans had a larger or equal number of metrics achieving their objectives as compared to clinical plans. By using the single-site or multi-site model, one, two, and one plans had improved metrics compared to clinical plans in the liver, bone, and lung testing datasets, respectively. For these patients, planning target volume (PTV)D99% met its objective in the three testing datasets whilst it was most 1.3% of the prescription dose lower than the dose limit in clinical plans. In all cases, the target coverage in AI-RTPlans reported in terms of PTVD95%, PTVD99%, or internal gross tumour volume (iGTV)mean was within ± 2% of the prescription dose relative to the clinical value for all plans. The target near-to-maximum dose measured with PTVD2% was within ± 2.9% of the prescription dose relative to the clinical value in all plans. All sites considered, the mean ± SD differences in CI100 and CI50 relative to the clinical value were 1.5 ± 2.7%/1.2 ± 2.5% and 1.6 ± 3.1%/1.4 ± 2.8% with the single-site models/multi-site model. Lastly, all evaluated OAR dose metrics were within ± 2.3 Gy of the clinical value in all AI-RTPlans.

Discussion

Combining all samples of each anatomical site to increase the sample size to train only one model provided satisfactory accuracy in the deep learning prediction of MU per CP of ablative hypofractionated radiotherapy of metastases. This strategy reduced the required computational resources and labour as compared with the case where models would have been trained for each anatomical site. Interestingly, massively augmented data used for training single-site models also provided satisfactory accuracy for the prediction of MU per CP. These findings are clinically significant to support same-day radiotherapy and to facilitate the planning of multiple metastases. It is worth noting that since the dose magnitude is proportional to the prescription dose and the MU per beam are proportional to the dose per fraction, many fractionation types were rescaled for training. The site with a single fractionation yielded the same level of accuracy as sites with multiple fractionations, validating our method. Furthermore, our approach is generalizable to diverse populations because the dose used as input is proportional to the patient attenuation. Based on our results, our method to predict the MU per CP can be integrated into the AI VMAT planning workflow to improve dose-volume metrics and dose conformity.

In each case considered, the dose-volume metrics and dose conformity obtained with AI-RTPlans were improved or comparable to the clinical dose-volume metrics and conformity. Similar results were obtained in the prediction of the MU per CP in lung cancer performed by our research group, for which the arc length was varied. These results suggest that the network architecture is robust in terms of anatomical site, prescription dose, and fractionation. Furthermore, in this current study, the agreement between calculated doses in AI-RTPlans and clinical plans was superior to an earlier study in conventional radiotherapy of prostate (average/minimum gamma passing rate 3%/3 mm = 98.8%/81.9% over all results of this study versus 92.0%/70.0% in the study for prostate cancer). It is worth noting that this current study only focused on the prediction of the MU per CP whilst the early study predicted both MLC leaf positions and MU per CP, which was more challenging.

The number of samples considered in this study was sufficient to predict the MU per CP accurately, but was obtained from a relatively small number of patients per site. Although data augmentation proved to be sufficient in this context, a larger patient cohort for each site may have provided more accurate predictions. The number of arcs was the same across all plans considered in this study. Further investigation into the effect of varying the number of arcs, or with intensity modulated radiotherapy plans, would be interesting. Furthermore, the results shown in this work were achieved with the most performant hyperspace parameter combination of the network. Other convolutional neural networks, such as the ResNet, or transformer-based network, or recurrent neural networks with long-short term memory layers able to consider the input’s temporal dependence, may have achieved higher accuracy in this context. Moreover, the workflow introduced in this work assumed that dose distributions per CP were available before training. In the routine clinical planning of AI VMAT, these dose distributions would be predicted for MLC leaf positions extraction, and therefore, the accuracy of the methods introduced in this work would depend on the accuracy of these predictions. Although AI VMAT planning is promising, several challenges hinder its clinical implementation. Consequently, improving the prediction accuracy of organ segmentation, 3D dose distribution, MLC leaf positions, and MU per CP is essential for widespread adoption.

limitations: The number of samples considered in this study was sufficient to predict the MU per CP accurately, but was obtained from a relatively small number of patients per site.
Although data augmentation proved to be sufficient in this context, a larger patient cohort for each site may have provided more accurate predictions.
research_gaps: Further investigation into the effect of varying the number of arcs, or with intensity modulated radiotherapy plans, would be interesting.

Other convolutional neural networks, such as the ResNet, or transformer-based network, or recurrent neural networks with long-short term memory layers able to consider the input’s temporal dependence, may have achieved higher accuracy in this context.
future_work: Improving the prediction accuracy of organ segmentation, 3D dose distribution, MLC leaf positions, and MU per CP is essential for widespread adoption.
methodological_weaknesses: The workflow introduced in this work assumed that dose distributions per CP were available before training, which may not be the case in routine clinical planning.
